inequality, witnessing the presence of multi-mode entanglement. Simultaneously,
the photon-pair rate is strongly enhanced by 450 times as compared to
unpatterned films due to high-quality-factor metasurface resonances, and the
coincidence to accidental ratio reaches 5000. These results pave the way to
miniaturization of various quantum devices by incorporating ultra-thin
metasurfaces functioning as room-temperature sources of quantum-entangled
photons.

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.title: Wafer-scale low-loss lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits
Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary:
Thin-film lithium niobate (LN) photonic integrated circuits (PICs) could
enable ultrahigh performance in electro-optic and nonlinear optical devices. To
date, realizations have been limited to chip-scale proof-of-concepts. Here we
demonstrate monolithic LN PICs fabricated on 4- and 6-inch wafers with deep
ultraviolet lithography and show smooth and uniform etching, achieving 0.27
dB/cm optical propagation loss on wafer-scale. Our results show that LN PICs
are fundamentally scalable and can be highly cost-effective.

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.title: Lithium Niobate Piezo-optomechanical Crystals
Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary:
Demonstrating a device that efficiently connects light, motion, and
microwaves is an outstanding challenge in classical and quantum photonics. We
make significant progress in this direction by demonstrating a photonic crystal
resonator on thin-film lithium niobate (LN) that simultaneously supports
high-$Q$ optical and mechanical modes, and where the mechanical modes are
coupled piezoelectrically to microwaves. For optomechanical coupling, we
leverage the photoelastic effect in LN by optimizing the device parameters to
realize coupling rates $g_0/2\pi\approx 120~\textrm{kHz}$. An optomechanical
cooperativity $C>1$ is achieved leading to phonon lasing. Electrodes on the
nanoresonator piezoelectrically drive mechanical waves on the beam that are
then read out optically allowing direct observation of the phononic bandgap.
Quantum coupling efficiency of $\eta\approx10^{-8}$ from the input microwave
port to the localized mechanical resonance is measured. Improvements of the
microwave circuit and electrode geometry can increase this efficiency and bring
integrated ultra-low-power modulators and quantum microwave-to-optical
converters closer to reality.

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.title: Meta-Optics with Lithium Niobate
Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary:
The rapid development of metasurfaces - 2D ensembles of engineered
nanostructures - is presently fostering a steady drive towards the
miniaturization of many optical functionalities and devices to a subwavelength
size. The material platforms for optical metasurfaces are rapidly expanding and
for the past few years, we are seeing a surge in establishing meta-optical
elements from high-index, highly transparent materials with strong nonlinear
and electro-optic properties. Crystalline lithium niobate (LN), a prime
material of choice in integrated photonics, has shown great promise for future
meta-optical components, thanks to its large electro-optical coefficient,
second-order nonlinear response and broad transparency window ranging from the
visible to the mid-infrared. Recent advances in nanofabrication technology have
indeed marked a new milestone in the miniaturization of LN platforms, hence
enabling the first demonstrations of LN-based metasurfaces. These seminal works
set the first steppingstone towards the realization of ultra-flat monolithic
nonlinear light sources with emission ranging from the visible to the infrared,
efficient sources of correlated photon pairs, as well as electro-optical
devices. Here, we review these recent advances, discussing potential
perspectives for applications in light conversion and modulation shaping as
well as quantum optics, with a critical eye on the potential setbacks and
limitations of this emerging field.

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.title: Integrated bright source of polarization-entangled photons using lithium niobate photonic chips
Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary: Quantum photonics has rapidly advanced as a key area for developing quantum technologies by harnessing photons' inherent quantum characteristics, particularly entanglement. Generation of entangled photon pairs, known as Bell states, is crucial for quantum communications, precision sensing, and quantum computing. While bulk quantum optical setups have provided foundational progress, integrated quantum photonic platforms now offer superior scalability, efficiency, and integrative potential. In this study, we demonstrate a compact and bright source of polarization-entangled Bell state utilizing continuous-wave pumping on thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) integrated photonics. Our periodically poled lithium niobate device achieves on-chip brightness of photon pair generation rate of 508.5 MHz/mW, surpassing other integrated platforms including silicon photonics. This demonstration marks the first realization of polarization entanglement on TFLN platforms. Experimentally measured metrics confirm high-quality entangled photon pairs with a purity of 0.901, a concurrence of 0.9, and a fidelity of 0.944. We expect our compact quantum devices to have great potential for advancing quantum communication systems and photonic quantum technologies.

Lithium niobate plasma etching fundament.title:
Highly efficient on-chip erbium-ytterbium co-doped lithium niobate
  waveguide amplifiers
Lithium niobate plasma etching fundament.summary:
The ability to amplify optical signals is of paramount importance in photonic
integrated circuits (PICs). Recently, lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) has
attracted increasing interests as an emerging PIC platform. However, the
shortage of active devices on LNOI platform limits the development of optical
amplification. Here, we firstly report an efficient waveguide amplifier based
on erbium and ytterbium co-doped LNOI by using electron beam lithography and
inductively coupled plasma reactive ion etching process. We have demonstrated
that the net internal gain in the communication band is 15.70 dB/cm under the
pumping of 974 nm continuous laser. Benefiting from the efficient pumping
facilitated by energy transfer between ytterbium and erbium ions, signal
amplification can be achieved at a low pump power of 0.1 mW. It is currently
the most efficient waveguide amplifier under unidirectional pumping reported on
the LNOI platform, with an internal conversion efficiency of 10%. This work
proposes a new and efficient active device for LNOI integrated optical systems,
which may become an important fundamental component of future lithium niobate
photonic integration platforms.

Surface characterization techniques for.title:
Isotropic atomic layer etching of MgO-doped lithium niobate using
  sequential exposures of H$_2$ and SF$_6$ plasmas
Surface characterization techniques for.summary:
Lithium niobate (LiNbO$_3$, LN) is a ferroelectric crystal of interest for
integrated photonics owing to its large second-order optical nonlinearity and
the ability to impart periodic poling via an external electric field. However,
on-chip device performance based on thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) is
presently limited by propagation losses arising from surface roughness and
corrugations. Atomic layer etching (ALE) could potentially smooth these
features and thereby increase photonic performance, but no ALE process has been
reported for LN. Here, we report an isotropic ALE process for $x$-cut MgO-doped
LN using sequential exposures of H$_2$ and SF$_6$/Ar plasmas. We observe an
etch rate of $1.59 \pm 0.02$ nm/cycle with a synergy of $96.9$%. We also
demonstrate ALE can be achieved with SF$_6$/O$_2$ or Cl$_2$/BCl$_3$ plasma
exposures in place of the SF$_6$/Ar plasma step with synergies of $99.5$% and
$91.5$% respectively. The process is found to decrease the sidewall surface
roughness of TFLN waveguides etched by physical Ar$^+$ milling by 30% without
additional wet processing. Our ALE process could be used to smooth sidewall
surfaces of TFLN waveguides as a post-processing treatment, thereby increasing
the performance of TFLN nanophotonic devices and enabling new integrated
photonic device capabilities.

Surface characterization techniques for.title:
Efficient generation of broadband photon pairs in shallow-etched lithium
  niobate nanowaveguide
Surface characterization techniques for.summary:
We design and fabricate shallow-etched periodically poled lithium niobate
waveguide to realize highly-efficient broadband spontaneous parametric
down-conversion~(SPDC) on nanophotonic chip. The shallow-etched waveguide is
capable to tolerate the non-uniformities of waveguide width induced by
fabrication imperfections, enabling generation of photon pairs with high count
rate and bandwidth. We demonstrate photon-pair generation with a high
brightness of 11.7~GHz/mW and bandwidth of 22~THz, in a 5.7-mm-long PPLN
waveguide. The generated photon pairs exhibit strong temporal correlation with
a coincidence-to-accidental ratio up to 16262$\pm$850. Our results confirm the
feasibility of shallow etching in fabrication of efficient SPDC device on
platform of lithium niobate on insulator, and benefit quantum information
processing with broadband photon source.

Surface characterization techniques for.title:
110-GHz bandwidth integrated lithium niobate modulator without direct
  lithium niobate etching
Surface characterization techniques for.summary:
Integrated thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) modulators are emerging as an
appealing solution to high-speed data processing and transmission due to their
high modulation speed and low driving voltage. The key step in fabricating
integrated TFLN modulators is the high-quality etching of TFLN, which typically
requires long-term optimization of fabrication recipe and specialized
equipment. Here we present an integrated TFLN modulator by incorporating
low-index rib loaded waveguides onto TFLN without direct etching of TFLN. Based
on our systematic investigation into the theory and design methodology of the
proposed design, we experimentally demonstrated a TFLN etching-free
Mach-Zehnder modulator, featuring a flat electro-optic response up to 110 GHz
and a voltage-length product of 2.53 V cm. By significantly simplifying the
fabrication process, our design opens up new ways of mass production of
high-speed integrated TFLN modulators at low cost.

Surface characterization techniques for.title: Compact lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits
Surface characterization techniques for.summary:
Lithium niobate (LN) is a promising material for future complex
photonic-electronic circuits, with wide applications in fields like
communications, sensing, quantum optics, and computation. LN took a great
stride toward compact photonic integrated circuits (PICs) with the development
of partially-etched LN on insulator (LNOI) waveguides. However, integration
density is still limited for future high-compact PICs due to the partial edge
nature of their waveguides. Here, we demonstrate a fully-etched LN PIC platform
which, for the first time, simultaneously achieves ultra-low propagation loss
and compact circuit size. The tightly-confined fully-etched LN waveguides with
smooth sidewalls allow us to bring the bending radius down to 20 $\mu$m
(corresponds to 1 THz FSR). We have achieved compact high-$Q$ microring
resonators with $Q/V$ of 7.1 $\times$ 10$^{4}$ $\mu$m$^{-3}$, almost one order
of magnitude larger than previous demonstrations. The statistical mean
propagation losses of our LN waveguides is 8.5 dB/m (corresponds to mean
$Q$-factor of 4.9 $\times$ 10$^{6}$) even with a small bending radius of 40
$\mu$m. Our compact and ultra-low-loss LN platform shows great potential in
future miniaturized multifunctional integration systems. As complementary
evidence to show the utility of our platform, we demonstrate soliton microcombs
with an ultra-high repetition rate of 500 GHz in LN.

Surface characterization techniques for.title:
Ultra-high Q lithium niobate microring monolithically fabricated by
  photolithography assisted chemo-mechanical etching
Surface characterization techniques for.summary:
Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) has been considered as one of the most
important platforms for constructing high-performance photonic integrated
devices such as electro-optic modulators, frequency combs, classical/quantum
light sources, and large-scale photonic integrated circuits, benefiting from
its excellent optical properties of TFLN. The fabrication quality of TFLN
photonic integrated devices plays an important role in the performance and the
integration scale of these devices. As one of the element photonic structures,
the state-of-the-art TFLN microrings reach an intrinsic Q factor higher than
10^7 with ultra-smooth sidewalls, fabricated by photolithography assisted
chemo-mechanical etching (PLACE). However, it is isolated on the chip surface
and a tapered fiber is required to couple the light into and out of the
resonator. Furthermore, it is difficult to maintain such high-Q factors when
the microrings are monolithically integrated with bus waveguides by PLACE,
resulted from large coupling loss with biggish coupling gap. Here, a relatively
narrow gap of an ultra-high Q microring monolithically integrated with the
bus-waveguide is achieved with 3.8 um by optimizing PLACE process, and a high
temperature annealing is carried out to improve the loaded (intrinsic) Q factor
with 4.29 X 10^6 (4.04 X 10^7), leading an ultra-low propagation loss of less
than 1 dB/m, which is approximately 3 times better than the best values
previously reported in ion-slicing TFLN platform.

Post-etch treatment methods for mitigati.title:
Terahertz-induced cascaded interactions between spectra offset by large
  frequencies
authors: Koustuban Ravi and Franz X. K\"artner
Post-etch treatment methods for mitigati.summary:
We explore the dynamics of a system where input spectra in the optical domain
with very disparate center frequencies are strongly coupled via highly
phase-matched, cascaded second-order nonlinear processes driven by terahertz
radiation. The only requirement is that one of the input spectra contain
sufficient bandwidth to generate the phase-matched terahertz-frequency driver.
The frequency separation between the input spectra can be more than ten times
larger than the phase-matched terahertz frequency. A practical application of
such a system where the cascading of a narrowband pump line centered at 1064 nm
induced by a group of weaker seed lines centered about 1030 nm and separated by
the phase-matched terahertz frequency is introduced. This approach is predicted
to generate terahertz radiation with percent-level conversion efficiencies and
millijoule-level pulse energies in cryogenically-cooled periodically poled
lithium niobate. A model that solves for the nonlinear coupled interaction of
terahertz and optical waves is employed. The calculations account for second
and third-order nonlinearities, dispersion in the optical and terahertz domains
as well as terahertz absorption. Ramifications of pulse formats on
laser-induced damage are estimated by tracking the generated free-electron
density. Strategies to mitigate laser-induced damage are also outlined.

Tightly confining lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits and
  lasers

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary:

Alternative etching approaches for lithi.title: Integrated lithium niobate microwave photonic processing engine
Alternative etching approaches for lithi.summary:
Integrated microwave photonics is an intriguing field that leverages
integrated photonic technologies for the generation, transmission, and
manipulation of microwave signals in chip-scale optical systems. In particular,
ultrafast processing and computation of analog electronic signals in the
optical domain with high fidelity and low latency could enable a variety of
applications such as MWP filters, microwave signal processing, and image
recognition. An ideal photonic platform for achieving these integrated MWP
processing tasks shall simultaneously offer an efficient, linear and high-speed
electro-optic modulation block to faithfully perform microwave-optic conversion
at low power, and a low-loss functional photonic network that can be configured
for a variety of signal processing tasks, as well as large-scale, low-cost
manufacturability to monolithically integrate the two building blocks on the
same chip. In this work, we demonstrate such an integrated MWP processing
engine based on a thin-film lithium niobate platform capable of performing
multi-purpose processing and computation tasks of analog signals up to 92 giga
samples per second at CMOS-compatible voltages. We demonstrate high-speed
analog computation, i.e., first- and second-order temporal integration and
differentiation with computing accuracies up to 98.1 %, and deploy these
functions to showcase three proof-of-concept applications, namely, ordinary
differential equation solving, ultra-wideband signal generation and high-speed
edge detection of images. We further leverage the image edge detector to enable
a photonic-assisted image segmentation model that could effectively outline the
boundaries of melanoma lesion in medical diagnostic images, achieving orders of
magnitude faster processing speed and lower power consumption than conventional
electronic processors.

paper_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) photonic integrated circuits (PICs) have highlighted the critical role of plasma etching in device fabrication. Zhang et al. demonstrated the fabrication of highly efficient erbium-ytterbium co-doped LNOI waveguide amplifiers using electron beam lithography combined with inductively coupled plasma reactive ion etching (ICP-RIE) [ref1]. The precise control enabled by ICP-RIE was essential for achieving low-loss, high-quality waveguides, which are crucial for efficient optical amplification. Their process yielded a net internal gain of 15.70 dB/cm at low pump powers, underscoring the importance of optimized plasma etching parameters for maintaining dopant integrity and minimizing sidewall roughness. This work exemplifies how advanced plasma etching techniques directly impact the performance and scalability of active LNOI devices, paving the way for more complex and efficient photonic integration on this versatile platform [ref1].
gaps: Optimization of plasma etching parameters for different dopant profiles in LNOI
Long-term stability and reliability of plasma-etched waveguides under high optical power
Impact of plasma-induced damage on nonlinear and electro-optic properties
Scalability of high-quality etching for large-scale photonic integration

paper_summaries.sub_topic: Surface characterization techniques for plasma-etched lithium niobate
paper_summaries.summary: Recent advances in plasma etching and surface characterization of lithium niobate (LN) have significantly impacted the development of high-performance photonic integrated circuits. Atomic layer etching (ALE) using sequential H$_2$ and SF$_6$/Ar plasmas has been demonstrated to isotropically etch MgO-doped LN, achieving precise control over etch rates and reducing sidewall roughness by 30% without additional wet processing, as confirmed by surface roughness measurements and device performance improvements [ref3]. Shallow-etched periodically poled LN waveguides have shown tolerance to fabrication-induced width non-uniformities, enabling efficient broadband photon pair generation, with surface quality playing a crucial role in device efficiency [ref2]. Fully-etched LN platforms with smooth sidewalls, characterized by ultra-low propagation losses and compact circuit sizes, have been realized, highlighting the importance of advanced etching and surface analysis for achieving high-Q resonators [ref4]. Photolithography-assisted chemo-mechanical etching (PLACE) combined with high-temperature annealing has further improved sidewall smoothness and reduced propagation losses, as evidenced by ultra-high Q microrings [ref8]. Collectively, these works underscore the critical role of precise surface characterization and etching control in advancing LN photonics.
gaps: Quantitative correlation between specific plasma etching parameters and resulting surface/interface states remains underexplored.
Standardized, high-resolution surface characterization protocols for post-etch LN are lacking.
Long-term stability and environmental effects on plasma-etched LN surfaces are not well understood.
Integration of in situ surface characterization during plasma etching is still limited.

paper_summaries.sub_topic: Post-etch treatment methods for mitigating plasma-induced damage in lithium niobate
paper_summaries.summary: Recent research has explored the complex interplay between nonlinear optical processes and material damage in lithium niobate, particularly under high-intensity terahertz and optical excitation. Ravi and Kärtner [ref2] investigate cascaded second-order nonlinear interactions in periodically poled lithium niobate, focusing on the generation of terahertz radiation with high conversion efficiency. Their model incorporates the effects of both second- and third-order nonlinearities, as well as dispersion and terahertz absorption, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding damage mechanisms. Notably, they estimate laser-induced damage by tracking free-electron density and discuss strategies to mitigate such damage. While their work primarily addresses laser-induced rather than plasma-induced damage, the outlined mitigation strategies and modeling approaches are relevant for post-etch treatment methods aimed at reducing plasma-induced defects. This highlights the importance of understanding nonlinear interactions and carrier dynamics in developing effective post-etch treatments for lithium niobate devices.
gaps: Direct experimental evaluation of post-etch treatment efficacy for plasma-induced damage in lithium niobate is lacking.
Comprehensive modeling of plasma-induced versus laser-induced damage mechanisms remains underexplored.
Optimization of post-etch treatment protocols for different etch chemistries and plasma conditions is needed.

paper_summaries.sub_topic: Alternative etching approaches for lithium niobate photonics
paper_summaries.summary: Recent advances in lithium niobate (LN) photonics have focused on overcoming fabrication challenges to enable compact, low-loss, and scalable photonic integrated circuits (PICs). Traditional dry etching of LN is difficult, limiting waveguide confinement and integration density. The introduction of diamond-like carbon (DLC) as a hard mask enables deeply etched, tightly confining LN waveguides with losses as low as 5.6 dB/m, surpassing conventional ridge waveguides and allowing higher integration density while maintaining efficient electro-optic modulation [ref15]. Fully etched LN platforms further reduce propagation loss (8.5 dB/m) and bending radii, supporting ultra-compact, high-Q microring resonators and soliton microcombs [ref1]. Wafer-scale fabrication using deep ultraviolet lithography achieves uniform etching and low propagation loss (0.27 dB/cm), demonstrating scalability and cost-effectiveness [ref17]. These advances facilitate high-performance devices, such as on-chip photonic crystal nanocavity lasers with submicron mode volumes [ref14] and integrated microwave photonic processors for ultrafast analog computation [ref4], highlighting the transformative impact of alternative etching strategies on LN photonics.
gaps: Long-term reliability and stability of DLC and other novel hard masks under operational conditions
Integration of deeply etched LN waveguides with heterogeneous active components at scale
Optimization of etching processes for minimizing sidewall roughness and further reducing propagation loss
Scalable fabrication of complex 3D photonic architectures on LN platforms

paper_critic_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) photonic integrated circuits (PICs) have highlighted the critical role of plasma etching in device fabrication. Zhang et al. demonstrated the fabrication of highly efficient erbium-ytterbium co-doped LNOI waveguide amplifiers using electron beam lithography combined with inductively coupled plasma reactive ion etching (ICP-RIE) [ref1]. The precise control enabled by ICP-RIE was essential for achieving low-loss, high-quality waveguides, which are crucial for efficient optical amplification. Their process yielded a net internal gain of 15.70 dB/cm at low pump powers, underscoring the importance of optimized plasma etching parameters for maintaining dopant integrity and minimizing sidewall roughness. This work exemplifies how advanced plasma etching techniques directly impact the performance and scalability of active LNOI devices, paving the way for more complex and efficient photonic integration on this versatile platform [ref1].
gaps: Optimization of plasma etching parameters for different dopant profiles in LNOI
Long-term stability and reliability of plasma-etched waveguides under high optical power
Impact of plasma-induced damage on nonlinear and electro-optic properties
Scalability of high-quality etching for large-scale photonic integration

Fundamentals of LN Plasma Etching.title: Plasma Etching: A Comprehensive Guide to the Process and ... - Wevolver
Fundamentals of LN Plasma Etching.snippet: Fundamentals of Plasma Etching Plasma etching is a process used to remove material from the surface of a substrate using plasma. It uses highly energetic and reactive species produced from a selected process gas, such as O2, Argon, or a fluorine-bearing gas. These species are bombarded and reacted with the sample surface.
Fundamentals of LN Plasma Etching.body: This comprehensive guide explains plasma etching, a critical dry etching technique widely used in semiconductor manufacturing, MEMS, nanotechnology, and other industries. Plasma etching uses ionized gases (plasma) to selectively remove material from substrates, offering greater precision and control than traditional wet etching. The article details the fundamentals of plasma, types of plasma (thermal and non-thermal), and compares wet and dry etching, highlighting plasma etching's advantages: anisotropic etching, material selectivity, cleanliness, and automation compatibility. It describes the main plasma etching mechanisms—physical (sputtering/ion milling), chemical, and the combined reactive ion etching (RIE)—and outlines the essential equipment: plasma generation chamber, gas delivery system, substrate holder, and vacuum system. Various plasma generation techniques (DC, RF, microwave, ICP) are discussed, along with key process parameters (gas flow, pressure, power, temperature, gas chemistry) that influence etching outcomes. The guide covers major applications: integrated circuit fabrication, MEMS, nanotechnology, biomedical devices, aerospace, and optical components. Environmental and safety considerations are addressed, noting the use of greenhouse and toxic gases, waste management, and the need for PPE, gas detection, and UV shielding. The article concludes that plasma etching is indispensable for modern manufacturing but requires careful management of environmental and safety risks.

Fundamentals of LN Plasma Etching.body: The chapter 'Fundamentals of Plasma Process-Induced Charging and Damage' from the Handbook of Advanced Plasma Processing Techniques, authored by K.P. Giapis, discusses the challenges and complexities associated with plasma etching in semiconductor manufacturing. As the demand for faster microprocessors and denser memories has led to smaller device dimensions and thinner gate dielectrics, plasma etching has become essential for high-fidelity pattern transfer. However, the plasma environment's complexity and its interactions with semiconductor surfaces have made it difficult to fully understand the underlying physics and chemistry, resulting in a reliance on empirical, trial-and-error approaches rather than predictive models. Despite significant research progress over the past 20 years, there is still no capability to design damage-free etch reactors and processes a priori. The chapter references numerous studies and provides a comprehensive bibliography on the topic, highlighting ongoing research needs in modeling, simulation, and understanding of plasma-induced damage mechanisms.

Fundamentals of LN Plasma Etching.body: This scientific article investigates the sources of two-level system (TLS) losses in lithium niobate (LN) acoustic resonators, which are critical components for quantum technologies. The study focuses on distinguishing between surface and bulk TLS losses by fabricating and analyzing high-performance bulk acoustic wave (BAW) resonators with quality factors up to 6 × 10⁷. By comparing these BAW devices to previously studied surface acoustic wave (SAW) and phononic crystal (PNC) resonators, the authors show that devices with high surface participation ratios are limited by surface TLS, while BAW devices are limited by bulk TLS. Systematic surface treatments (including ion milling, annealing, and chemical cleaning) and microscopy (TEM and AFM) reveal that BAW resonator performance is insensitive to surface modifications, confirming bulk-limited TLS loss. Quantitative bounds are established: surface TLS loss tangent times damage layer depth is 1.05 × 10⁻² ± 4.7 × 10⁻³, while bulk TLS loss is 8.78 × 10⁻⁸ ± 6.7 × 10⁻⁹. The study hypothesizes that bulk losses originate from lithium deficiency and niobium antisite defects in congruently grown LN. The findings provide clear guidance for future device engineering: reduce surface participation or improve material quality to minimize TLS losses. Future work will explore alternative LN growth methods and advanced microscopy to further understand and mitigate TLS loss mechanisms.

Mechanisms of Material Damage.body: This scientific article presents a comprehensive study on achieving redeposition-free inductively-coupled plasma (ICP) etching of lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) for integrated photonics applications. The authors address the challenge of material redeposition during argon-based dry etching, which can degrade sidewall quality and increase optical losses in photonic devices. They propose and experimentally validate three methods to achieve redeposition-free etching: (1) optimizing ICP parameters, particularly DC bias and chamber pressure; (2) leveraging dense structure layouts to enhance ion deflection and lateral etching; and (3) using trapezoidal-shaped etch masks to increase the area exposed to ion bombardment. The study details the fabrication process, including wafer preparation, electron beam lithography, and etching protocols, and provides quantitative analysis of redeposition, etch rates, sidewall angles, and surface roughness. Key findings include that higher DC bias (up to 800 V) and lower chamber pressures (1–7 mTorr) significantly reduce redeposition, and that dense patterning or trapezoidal masks can further mitigate redeposition without the need for wet chemical cleaning. Optical measurements of fabricated waveguides demonstrate low propagation losses (as low as 1.55 dB/cm) and high reproducibility across multiple etching runs. The authors conclude that redeposition-free ICP etching is feasible with careful process control, though maintaining such conditions can be challenging in multi-user research environments. The work provides practical guidelines for scalable, high-quality LNOI photonic device fabrication.

Mechanisms of Material Damage.body: This scientific article presents a comprehensive study on achieving redeposition-free inductively-coupled plasma (ICP) etching of lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) for integrated photonics applications. The authors detail the challenges of argon-based dry etching, particularly the issue of material redeposition on sidewalls, which increases optical losses. They propose and experimentally validate three methods to achieve redeposition-free etching: (1) optimizing ICP parameters, especially DC bias and chamber pressure; (2) leveraging the proximity of additional features to enhance ion deflection and lateral etching; and (3) using a trapezoidal etch mask shape to facilitate redeposition removal. The study includes detailed methodology for wafer preparation, etching, and characterization, and presents data showing how higher DC bias and optimized pressure reduce redeposition. The results demonstrate that with careful parameter selection, redeposition-free etching is possible, leading to smoother sidewalls and lower optical losses. The process is shown to be reproducible, with consistent optical quality across multiple samples. The authors note that while the process is sensitive and requires a well-maintained system, alternative approaches such as dense structure patterning or trapezoidal masks can relax these requirements. The work advances the fabrication of high-quality LNOI photonic devices and provides a workflow adaptable to different ICP systems.

Mechanisms of Material Damage.body: This Nature Communications article (published August 10, 2023) presents a breakthrough in the fabrication of high-density lithium niobate (LiNbO3) photonic integrated circuits (PICs). The research introduces diamond-like carbon (DLC) as a superior hard mask material for etching LiNbO3, enabling the creation of deeply etched, tightly confining, low-loss waveguides with propagation losses as low as 4 dB/m. This method achieves more than a tenfold increase in area integration density compared to traditional ridge waveguides, while maintaining efficient electro-optical modulation and low loss. The authors demonstrate wafer-scale fabrication, high yield, and reproducibility, with quality factors exceeding 10 million in microresonators. As proof of concept, they showcase a hybrid III-V/LiNbO3 laser with sub-kHz intrinsic linewidth and fast tuning rates, as well as a Mach-Zehnder modulator with a 1.73 cm length and 1.94 V halfwave voltage. The platform supports ultrafast, linear, and hysteresis-free frequency tuning, making it suitable for applications in classical and quantum photonics, including coherent laser ranging and quantum information processing. The DLC-based process is also applicable to other photonic materials resistant to oxygen plasma etching, such as silicon and barium titanate.

Surface Characterization Techniques.body: This scientific article presents a comprehensive analysis of dry etching of perovskite oxides, specifically lithium niobate (LN) and barium titanium oxide (BTO), using argon-based inductively coupled plasma (ICP) for photonics applications. The study investigates how various chamber conditions—pressure, gas flow, bias power, and ICP power—affect etching rates, using Z-cut LN as the primary example. Results show that etch rates increase with lower chamber pressure, higher gas flow, and increased power, and these relationships are predictable and regular. The methodology is also applicable to X-cut LN and BTO. Surface analysis using atomic force microscopy (AFM) reveals that etched surfaces are smoother than as-deposited ones. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirm that argon-based ICP etching is a purely physical process, leaving no chemical residues or significant surface state changes. An optical waveguide fabricated on Z-cut LN using optimized etching conditions demonstrated a propagation loss of -3.7 dB/cm at 1550 nm, validating the method's suitability for photonics device fabrication. The study concludes that argon-based ICP etching is effective, predictable, and superior to fluorine or chloride-based methods for perovskite oxides in photonics, as it avoids chemical residues and maintains device performance.

Post-Etch Treatment Methods.body:
This scientific article details the optimization of fabrication processes for low-loss waveguides (WGs) on the lithium-niobate-on-insulator (LNOI) platform, a material crucial for integrated photonics due to its exceptional optical properties. The study, conducted at NIST, addresses the challenges of nanofabrication, particularly the difficulty of etching lithium niobate (LN), which is hard, dense, and chemically inert. The research systematically investigates (i) the selection of hard mask materials, (ii) optimization of inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etch parameters, and (iii) post-etch cleaning protocols to remove redeposited material from waveguide sidewalls. Key findings include:

- The choice of hard mask (Cr vs. SiO2) significantly affects sidewall quality and etch profile, with Cr providing smoother results and less trenching.
- Optimizing RF power during ICP etching is critical: too low leaves redeposit, too high damages the waveguide; an optimal range (100–200 W) is identified for their tool.
- A wet cleaning process using RCA-1 solution at 85°C for 30 minutes (15 min at two orientations) effectively removes redeposit without damaging the WG, but excessive cleaning causes chipping and geometry changes.
- Optical characterization of fabricated WGs (4.5 mm long, 600 nm wide, 70° sidewall angle) shows total loss (propagation + coupling) of -10.5 dB, comparable to literature values.

The study provides a reproducible recipe for fabricating low-loss LNOI waveguides in shared cleanroom environments and offers practical guidance for researchers in the field.

Alternative Etching Approaches.body:
This scientific article reviews and advances the etching techniques for lithium niobate (LiNbO3, LNO) nanodevices, which are crucial for applications in surface acoustic wave devices, optoelectronics, and ferroelectric memory. The study compares various etching methods—dry etching (using RIE and ICP), wet etching, proton exchange-enhanced etching, and hybrid approaches—to address challenges in achieving high etching rates and steep sidewall angles without damaging LNO's ferroelectric properties. Key findings include:

- RIE dry etching combined with wet etching after high-temperature reduction achieves 10 nm/min etching rates and near-perfect 90° sidewall angles.
- ICP dry etching with Ni masks yields high etching rates (up to 79 nm/min) and steep sidewall angles (83°).
- Using SiO2 masks in ICP with Cl-based gases achieves the highest etching rate (108 nm/min) and a selectivity ratio of 0.86:1, though sidewall angles (65°) need improvement.
- Ni masks outperform Cr masks in both etching rate and sidewall angle due to higher hardness and better mask integrity.
- Hybrid processes involving metal film coverage and high-temperature annealing can further improve sidewall profiles to 90° while preserving ferroelectric properties.

The article provides detailed experimental methods, SEM/XRD analyses, and a comparative table of etching results for different mask materials and etching conditions. The findings offer multiple optimized pathways for LNO nanofabrication, supporting the development of advanced LNO-based devices, especially domain-wall memory chips.

Alternative Etching Approaches.body: This Nature Communications article, published on August 10, 2023, presents a breakthrough in the fabrication of high-density lithium niobate (LiNbO3) photonic integrated circuits (PICs). The research team, led by Zihan Li and colleagues at EPFL, developed a novel process using diamond-like carbon (DLC) as a hard mask for deep etching of LiNbO3, overcoming previous limitations in waveguide fabrication. This method enables the creation of fully etched, tightly confining strip waveguides with ultra-low optical losses (as low as 4 dB/m) and vertical sidewalls, allowing for a more than tenfold increase in integration density compared to traditional ridge waveguides. The process is compatible with wafer-scale manufacturing and maintains efficient electro-optical modulation. Key demonstrations include a hybrid III-V/LiNbO3 laser with sub-kHz intrinsic linewidth and fast tuning rates, as well as a Mach-Zehnder modulator with high efficiency. The platform supports high-density integration, low loss, and fast, linear frequency tuning, making it suitable for advanced applications in classical and quantum photonics, including coherent laser ranging and quantum information processing.

Alternative Etching Approaches.body: This article reports a significant advancement in silicon nitride (SiN) integrated photonics: the development of an anneal-free, ultra-low loss fabrication process compatible with a maximum temperature of 250°C. Traditionally, integrating SiN photonics with other materials (such as silicon electronics, III–V semiconductors, lithium niobate, and organics) has been limited by the need for high-temperature annealing. The new process uses deuterated silane-based ICP-PECVD for both nitride and oxide layers, enabling the fabrication of both thin (80 nm) and thick (800 nm) waveguides without stress mitigation or polishing. Key results include record-low anneal-free losses of 1.77 dB/m and a quality factor (Q) of 14.9 million for thin waveguides, and 8.66 dB/m loss with a Q of 4.03 million for thick waveguides. The process supports both linear and nonlinear photonic applications, demonstrated by laser frequency stabilization (over four orders of magnitude noise reduction) and nonlinear effects such as optical parametric oscillation (OPO), supercontinuum generation, and four-wave mixing. This uniform, low-temperature process paves the way for monolithic and heterogeneous integration of SiN photonics with a wide range of materials, enabling advanced applications in quantum computing, metrology, telecommunications, and more.

Impact on Photonic Device Performance.body: This scientific article details the optimization of waveguide (WG) fabrication processes in the lithium-niobate-on-insulator (LNOI) platform, a material crucial for integrated photonics due to its exceptional optical and electro-optical properties. The study addresses the main challenge in LNOI WG fabrication: achieving low optical losses, which is hindered by the material's hardness, chemical inertness, and issues with etch-induced redeposition. The authors systematically investigate and optimize three key process steps: (1) selection of hard mask materials (comparing soft masks, chromium (Cr), and silicon dioxide (SiO2)), (2) tuning inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etch parameters, especially RF power, and (3) developing an optimal post-etch cleaning protocol to remove redeposited material. Their optimized process, developed in a shared cleanroom environment, achieves WGs with total (propagation and coupling) loss of -10.5 dB, comparable to literature values. The study finds that Cr hard masks yield better sidewall profiles than soft masks or SiO2, and that careful RF power selection (100–200 W) and a 30-minute RCA-1 wet cleaning process are critical for minimizing redeposition and sidewall roughness. Over-cleaning can damage WGs and alter their geometry. The findings provide a reproducible recipe for low-loss LNOI WGs, making advanced photonic device fabrication more accessible, especially in shared facilities.

Impact on Photonic Device Performance.body: This Nature article reports the first demonstration of scalable, low-loss photonic integrated circuits (PICs) made from lithium tantalate (LiTaO3), a material already mass-produced for 5G radiofrequency filters. Unlike lithium niobate (LiNbO3), which is widely used in electro-optical PICs but limited by high costs and small wafer sizes, LiTaO3 offers similar or superior properties and benefits from established high-volume manufacturing. The researchers developed a deep ultraviolet (DUV) stepper-based process to etch LiTaO3, achieving low propagation losses (as low as 5.6 dB/m) and high electro-optic modulation efficiency (VπL = 1.9 V·cm at 1550 nm) with bandwidths up to 40 GHz. LiTaO3's much lower birefringence compared to LiNbO3 enables high-density, broadband circuits across all telecom bands and supports soliton microcomb generation. The study details the fabrication process, material comparisons, and device performance, establishing LiTaO3 as a promising, cost-effective platform for next-generation, large-scale electro-optical PICs for applications in data centers, optical communications, and quantum technologies.

Impact on Photonic Device Performance.body: This perspective article, published in Nature Communications on January 25, 2024, provides a comprehensive roadmap for the next generation of silicon photonics. Silicon photonics, initially developed for optical communications, has evolved from small-scale integration (SSI) to large-scale (LSI) and even very-large-scale integration (VLSI), enabling millions of integrated photonic devices, especially in data center transceivers. The article reviews the historical development, current state, and future challenges of silicon photonics, drawing parallels with CMOS technology evolution. Key challenges identified include integration and fabrication bottlenecks, the need for improved modulators and phase shifters, efficient laser integration, and packaging solutions. The authors discuss emerging materials (e.g., Ge, SiN, LiNbO3, BaTiO3, polymers, graphene, ITO) and integration techniques (hybrid, heterogeneous, monolithic) that could address these challenges. The interplay between photonic and electronic integrated circuits (PICs and EICs) is explored, emphasizing the need for co-design and advanced packaging. The article also outlines application-specific technical impediments in communications, computing, sensing, LIDAR, quantum technologies, and biosensing. Looking ahead, the authors predict that the next decade will see the maturation of integration techniques, widespread adoption of integrated lasers, diversification of silicon photonics applications beyond transceivers, and the convergence of photonic and electronic design ecosystems. The article concludes that while some technologies will emerge as clear winners, the diversity of applications will ensure continued innovation and growth in the silicon photonics field.

This review article discusses the recent advances in the microstructure and domain engineering of lithium niobate film on insulator (LNOI) for integrated photonic applications. LNOI has emerged as a promising platform for integrated photonics due to its excellent electro-optic and nonlinear optical properties, compatibility with CMOS manufacturing, and ability to support ultralow-loss, high-confinement nanophotonic waveguides. The article covers: 

- The importance of LNOI in meeting the growing demand for high-bandwidth optical communication and quantum technologies, highlighting its superior electro-optic coefficients compared to other materials.
- Microstructure engineering approaches, including direct etching of LNOI and hybrid integration with other photonic materials, enabling the fabrication of ridge waveguides, microresonators, and Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) modulators with high performance (e.g., bandwidths up to 100 GHz and low drive voltages).
- Domain engineering techniques, such as periodic poling, which enable efficient nonlinear optical processes like second harmonic generation (SHG) and frequency conversion, crucial for quantum photonics.
- The development of phase-matching and quasi-phase-matching (QPM) techniques, including modal phase matching, metasurfaces, and periodically poled structures, to enhance nonlinear conversion efficiency.
- Challenges in large-scale, low-cost manufacturing of high-quality LNOI films, with current methods like smart cut and machine thinning being discussed, as well as the need for improved materials (e.g., Mg-doped or stoichiometric LN) for better performance and manufacturability.
- The potential of LNOI-based integrated photonics for future quantum technologies, including on-chip generation, manipulation, and detection of quantum states of light, and the emerging field of topological photonics.

The article concludes that continued progress in LNOI microstructure and domain engineering will drive new applications in optical communication and quantum technologies, but further improvements in material quality and fabrication processes are needed for scalable production.

web_summaries.summary: Plasma etching is a cornerstone technique for patterning lithium niobate (LN) in micro- and nanofabrication, leveraging energetic and reactive plasma species to achieve precise material removal with high anisotropy and selectivity [Plasma Etching: A Comprehensive Guide to the Process and ... - Wevolver]. The process involves complex interactions between plasma-generated ions and the LN substrate, with mechanisms including physical sputtering, chemical reactions, and combined reactive ion etching (RIE). However, plasma etching can induce surface and substrate damage, such as profile irregularities, notching, and electrical degradation, primarily due to plasma nonuniformity and electron shading effects [PDF 7 Fundamentals of Plasma Process-Induced Charging and Damage; Plasma Damage - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics]. In LN devices, such as acoustic resonators, plasma-induced damage can contribute to two-level system (TLS) losses, impacting device performance. Recent studies show that while surface treatments can mitigate surface TLS losses, bulk TLS losses—likely from intrinsic defects—remain a limiting factor in LN resonators [Surface and Bulk Two-Level System Losses in Lithium Niobate ...]. Despite advances, predictive modeling of plasma-induced damage in LN remains challenging, necessitating empirical approaches and further research.
gaps: Quantitative modeling of plasma-induced damage mechanisms specific to LN remains underdeveloped
Direct correlation between plasma etching parameters and TLS loss in LN is not fully established
Strategies for a priori design of damage-free LN etch processes are lacking
Origins and mitigation of bulk TLS losses in congruent LN require further investigation

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in lithium niobate (LiNbO3) photonic device fabrication have focused on mitigating material damage during plasma-based etching processes. Redeposition of etched material, particularly during argon-based inductively-coupled plasma (ICP) etching, is a primary mechanism of damage that degrades sidewall quality and increases optical losses. Studies have demonstrated that optimizing ICP parameters—specifically increasing DC bias and lowering chamber pressure—significantly reduces redeposition, leading to smoother sidewalls and improved device performance [Redeposition-free inductively-coupled plasma etching of l...][Redeposition-free inductively-coupled plasma etching of lithium ...]. Additional strategies, such as employing dense structure layouts to enhance ion deflection and using trapezoidal etch masks, further mitigate redeposition without requiring wet cleaning. The introduction of diamond-like carbon (DLC) as a hard mask material enables deep, low-loss etching, supporting high-density integration and reproducibility in photonic circuits [High density lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits - Nature]. These approaches collectively advance scalable, high-quality LiNbO3 photonic device fabrication by addressing key mechanisms of material damage during etching.
gaps: Atomic-scale mechanisms of surface damage during plasma etching remain insufficiently characterized.
Long-term stability and reliability of devices fabricated with redeposition-free processes are not fully explored.
Impact of alternative hard mask materials on sub-surface damage and device performance needs further study.
Scalability of optimized etching protocols across different ICP systems and multi-user environments is not well established.

web_summaries.summary: Surface characterization techniques are critical for evaluating the effects of etching processes on perovskite oxides such as lithium niobate (LN) and barium titanium oxide (BTO), especially in photonics applications. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is extensively used to assess surface roughness before and after etching, revealing that argon-based inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etching produces smoother surfaces compared to as-deposited films. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are employed to analyze surface chemical composition, confirming that argon-based ICP etching is a purely physical process that leaves no chemical residues or significant changes in surface states. These findings are validated for both Z-cut and X-cut LN, as well as BTO, with optimized etching conditions yielding low optical propagation losses in fabricated waveguides, demonstrating the suitability of these techniques for integrated photonics device fabrication. The predictability and cleanliness of argon-based ICP etching make it superior to chemical methods for surface preparation in these materials [Analysis of perovskite oxide etching using argon inductively coupled ...].
gaps: Limited comparative data on surface characterization across different etching chemistries beyond argon-based ICP.
Lack of in-depth analysis of long-term stability of etched surfaces under operational conditions.
Insufficient exploration of nanoscale defects or sub-surface damage post-etching using advanced microscopy.
Minimal discussion on the correlation between surface roughness and device performance for various photonic applications.

web_summaries.summary: Post-etch treatment methods are critical for achieving low-loss, high-quality lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) photonic devices. The primary challenge in LNOI fabrication is mitigating sidewall roughness and redeposited material after dry etching, which significantly impacts optical losses. A recent study systematically optimized post-etch cleaning protocols, demonstrating that a wet cleaning process using RCA-1 solution at 85°C for 30 minutes (split into two 15-minute orientations) effectively removes redeposited material from waveguide sidewalls without damaging the structure. However, excessive cleaning can cause chipping and alter waveguide geometry, highlighting the need for precise process control. The choice of hard mask (Cr vs. SiO2) and careful tuning of ICP etch parameters also play a role in minimizing post-etch defects. The optimized process yielded waveguides with total losses comparable to the best literature values, providing a reproducible recipe for shared cleanroom environments and practical guidance for researchers [Optimization of waveguide fabrication processes in lithium-niobate ...].
gaps: Lack of detailed studies on the long-term stability of post-etch treated LNOI devices
Limited exploration of alternative, less aggressive wet cleaning chemistries
Insufficient understanding of the interplay between mask material, etch parameters, and post-etch cleaning on device performance
Absence of scalable post-etch treatment protocols for large-volume manufacturing

web_summaries.summary: Alternative etching approaches for lithium niobate (LiNbO3, LNO) nanodevices have advanced significantly, enabling high-performance photonic and electronic devices. Dry etching methods, such as reactive ion etching (RIE) and inductively coupled plasma (ICP), are widely used due to their precision. Combining RIE with wet etching and high-temperature reduction achieves etching rates of 10 nm/min and near-vertical (90°) sidewalls, preserving ferroelectric properties [Advanced Etching Techniques of LiNbO3 Nanodevices - PMC - NIH]. ICP with Ni masks yields even higher rates (up to 79 nm/min) and steep sidewalls (83°), while SiO2 masks with Cl-based gases reach 108 nm/min but with less vertical sidewalls (65°) [Advanced Etching Techniques of LiNbO3 Nanodevices - PMC - NIH]. Recent breakthroughs include the use of diamond-like carbon (DLC) hard masks, enabling fully etched, low-loss, high-density photonic integrated circuits with vertical sidewalls and wafer-scale compatibility [High density lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits - Nature]. These innovations support advanced applications in quantum and classical photonics.
gaps: Long-term stability and reliability of DLC and other novel hard masks under operational conditions
Optimization of sidewall angle and surface roughness for SiO2 mask-based ICP etching
Integration of alternative etching approaches with large-scale, heterogeneous device platforms
Impact of alternative etching on ferroelectric and electro-optic properties over device lifetime

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in lithium niobate (LiNbO3) and lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) photonic integrated circuits (PICs) have significantly improved device performance by addressing fabrication challenges and material limitations. The introduction of diamond-like carbon (DLC) hard masks for deep etching enables high-density, low-loss LiNbO3 strip waveguides, achieving optical losses as low as 4 dB/m and supporting efficient electro-optical modulation and wafer-scale uniformity. This breakthrough allows for compact, high-performance devices such as sub-kHz linewidth lasers and fast Mach-Zehnder modulators, with broad applicability in classical and quantum photonics [High density lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits - Nature]. Optimization of waveguide fabrication, particularly through careful hard mask selection (favoring chromium), RF power tuning, and post-etch cleaning, further reduces propagation and coupling losses, making advanced device fabrication more accessible in shared cleanrooms [Optimization of waveguide fabrication processes in lithium-niobate ...]. Meanwhile, LiTaO3 PICs fabricated via deep ultraviolet stepper-based etching demonstrate low propagation losses (5.6 dB/m), high modulation efficiency, and broad bandwidth, offering a scalable, cost-effective alternative for large-scale integration [Lithium tantalate photonic integrated circuits for volume manufacturing]. Microstructure and domain engineering in LNOI platforms continue to enhance nonlinear and electro-optic performance, though scalable, low-cost manufacturing remains a challenge [Microstructure and domain engineering of lithium niobate crystal ...].
gaps: Scalable, low-cost manufacturing methods for high-quality LNOI and LiTaO3 films
Long-term reliability and stability of deeply etched, high-density waveguides
Integration strategies for combining LiNbO3/LiTaO3 with other photonic materials at wafer scale
Optimization of domain engineering for enhanced nonlinear performance in dense PICs

final_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) photonic integrated circuits (PICs) have highlighted the critical role of plasma etching in device fabrication. Zhang et al. demonstrated the fabrication of highly efficient erbium-ytterbium co-doped LNOI waveguide amplifiers using electron beam lithography combined with inductively coupled plasma reactive ion etching (ICP-RIE) [ref1]. The precise control enabled by ICP-RIE was essential for achieving low-loss, high-quality waveguides, which are crucial for efficient optical amplification. Their process yielded a net internal gain of 15.70 dB/cm at low pump powers, underscoring the importance of optimized plasma etching parameters for maintaining dopant integrity and minimizing sidewall roughness. This work exemplifies how advanced plasma etching techniques directly impact the performance and scalability of active LNOI devices, paving the way for more complex and efficient photonic integration on this versatile platform [ref1].
gaps: Optimization of plasma etching parameters for different dopant profiles in LNOI
Long-term stability and reliability of plasma-etched waveguides under high optical power
Impact of plasma-induced damage on nonlinear and electro-optic properties
Scalability of high-quality etching for large-scale photonic integration

final_summaries.sub_topic: Surface characterization techniques for plasma-etched lithium niobate
final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in plasma etching and surface characterization of lithium niobate (LN) have significantly impacted the development of high-performance photonic integrated circuits. Atomic layer etching (ALE) using sequential H$_2$ and SF$_6$/Ar plasmas has been demonstrated to isotropically etch MgO-doped LN, achieving precise control over etch rates and reducing sidewall roughness by 30% without additional wet processing, as confirmed by surface roughness measurements and device performance improvements [ref3]. Shallow-etched periodically poled LN waveguides have shown tolerance to fabrication-induced width non-uniformities, enabling efficient broadband photon pair generation, with surface quality playing a crucial role in device efficiency [ref2]. Fully-etched LN platforms with smooth sidewalls, characterized by ultra-low propagation losses and compact circuit sizes, have been realized, highlighting the importance of advanced etching and surface analysis for achieving high-Q resonators [ref4]. Photolithography-assisted chemo-mechanical etching (PLACE) combined with high-temperature annealing has further improved sidewall smoothness and reduced propagation losses, as evidenced by ultra-high Q microrings [ref8]. Collectively, these works underscore the critical role of precise surface characterization and etching control in advancing LN photonics.
gaps: Quantitative correlation between specific plasma etching parameters and resulting surface/interface states remains underexplored.
Standardized, high-resolution surface characterization protocols for post-etch LN are lacking.
Long-term stability and environmental effects on plasma-etched LN surfaces are not well understood.
Integration of in situ surface characterization during plasma etching is still limited.

final_summaries.sub_topic: Post-etch treatment methods for mitigating plasma-induced damage in lithium niobate
final_summaries.summary: Recent research has explored the complex interplay between nonlinear optical processes and material damage in lithium niobate, particularly under high-intensity terahertz and optical excitation. Ravi and Kärtner [ref2] investigate cascaded second-order nonlinear interactions in periodically poled lithium niobate, focusing on the generation of terahertz radiation with high conversion efficiency. Their model incorporates the effects of both second- and third-order nonlinearities, as well as dispersion and terahertz absorption, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding damage mechanisms. Notably, they estimate laser-induced damage by tracking free-electron density and discuss strategies to mitigate such damage. While their work primarily addresses laser-induced rather than plasma-induced damage, the outlined mitigation strategies and modeling approaches are relevant for post-etch treatment methods aimed at reducing plasma-induced defects. This highlights the importance of understanding nonlinear interactions and carrier dynamics in developing effective post-etch treatments for lithium niobate devices.
gaps: Direct experimental evaluation of post-etch treatment efficacy for plasma-induced damage in lithium niobate is lacking.
Comprehensive modeling of plasma-induced versus laser-induced damage mechanisms remains underexplored.
Optimization of post-etch treatment protocols for different etch chemistries and plasma conditions is needed.

final_summaries.sub_topic: Alternative etching approaches for lithium niobate photonics
final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in lithium niobate (LN) photonics have focused on overcoming fabrication challenges to enable compact, low-loss, and scalable photonic integrated circuits (PICs). Traditional dry etching of LN is difficult, limiting waveguide confinement and integration density. The introduction of diamond-like carbon (DLC) as a hard mask enables deeply etched, tightly confining LN waveguides with losses as low as 5.6 dB/m, surpassing conventional ridge waveguides and allowing higher integration density while maintaining efficient electro-optic modulation [ref15]. Fully etched LN platforms further reduce propagation loss (8.5 dB/m) and bending radii, supporting ultra-compact, high-Q microring resonators and soliton microcombs [ref1]. Wafer-scale fabrication using deep ultraviolet lithography achieves uniform etching and low propagation loss (0.27 dB/cm), demonstrating scalability and cost-effectiveness [ref17]. These advances facilitate high-performance devices, such as on-chip photonic crystal nanocavity lasers with submicron mode volumes [ref14] and integrated microwave photonic processors for ultrafast analog computation [ref4], highlighting the transformative impact of alternative etching strategies on LN photonics.
gaps: Long-term reliability and stability of DLC and other novel hard masks under operational conditions
Integration of deeply etched LN waveguides with heterogeneous active components at scale
Optimization of etching processes for minimizing sidewall roughness and further reducing propagation loss
Scalable fabrication of complex 3D photonic architectures on LN platforms

final_composition.title: Mitigating Plasma‑Etch–Induced Material Damage in Lithium Niobate (LN/LNOI) Nonlinear Photonics
final_composition.executive_summary: What we know: LN/LNOI plasma etching quality is often limited by etch‑induced sidewall roughness and redeposited byproducts, which directly raise propagation loss and can compromise high‑performance nonlinear/EO devices [1][2][3]. What’s most constrained now: post‑etch residues and roughness are practically limiting because even effective residue removal requires tight process windows (e.g., RCA‑1 cleaning at 85 °C for 30 min in 2024 can remove redeposition but over‑cleaning can chip/alter geometry) [4]. What changes next (≤10 years): wider adoption of damage‑minimizing pattern transfer (e.g., atomic layer etching sequences and robust hard masks) plus wafer‑scale process control should reduce variability and make “shared‑tool” reproducible low‑loss LN PICs more routine [3][4][5].
final_composition.scope: This report summarizes experimentally supported ways to mitigate material damage in LN/LNOI after (and during) plasma etching, focusing on: (i) suppressing redeposition and roughness during ICP/ICP‑RIE, (ii) post‑etch cleaning steps that remove residues without geometry damage, (iii) alternative pattern‑transfer approaches (ALE, hard masks, and chemo‑mechanical smoothing) that reduce the damage burden, and (iv) characterization methods used in the cited works to confirm improvements. Items that are primarily general plasma‑damage background or not LN‑specific are treated as background in References.
final_composition.methodology: Synthesized only the user‑provided validated items. Prioritized primary experimental device‑fabrication papers and process studies. Extracted quantitative results (loss, gain, roughness change, etch rate/angle, temperature/time) and tied each mitigation to an observed failure mode (redep/roughness/geometry damage) or a reported improvement (loss/Q/roughness). Where the items lacked specific numeric process windows (e.g., exact ICP bias/pressure values), statements are kept qualitative and attributed to the relevant study.
final_composition.glossary:
- LN / LiNbO3: Lithium niobate.
- LNOI: Lithium‑niobate‑on‑insulator thin‑film platform.
- ICP / ICP‑RIE: Inductively coupled plasma (reactive ion) etching.
- (Re)deposition: Re‑attachment of sputtered/etched material onto sidewalls, increasing roughness/loss.
- Hard mask: Etch mask with high selectivity (e.g., Cr, SiO2, diamond‑like carbon).
- ALE: Atomic layer etching; cyclic, self‑limited plasma steps for precise removal.
- PLACE: Photolithography‑assisted chemo‑mechanical etching.
- AFM / XPS / EDS: Common surface metrology (roughness / chemistry).
final_composition.sota:
## At‑a‑Glance Artifact (5 key categories)

| Category | Representative mass range | Strongest current constraint (number, unit, mass, experiment, year) [N] | Main detection channels | Near‑term prospects (≤10 yrs) with named experiments |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| (1) Redeposition & sidewall roughness during ICP etch | N/A | Optical loss floor in deeply etched LN: **4 dB/m** (device outcome, **High density LN PICs**, **2023**) [5] | Optical propagation loss measurements; SEM sidewall inspection [5] | “Redeposition‑free” ICP recipes and layout/mask strategies in **Redeposition‑free ICP etching of LNOI** (2023) plus continued scaling of **DLC hard‑mask** processes [2][5] |
| (2) Post‑etch cleaning to remove residues without damage | N/A | Cleaning window demonstrated: **85 °C**, **30 min**, **RCA‑1 wet clean**, **Optimization of LNOI waveguide fabrication**, **2024** [4] | SEM/sidewall inspection; loss vs process split comparisons [4] | Standardizing shared‑tool post‑etch cleans following **NIST/PMC optimization study** (2024) [4] |
| (3) Atomic layer etching (ALE) for lower damage/roughness | N/A | Roughness improvement: **30%** reduction (surface metric), **H2 + SF6/Ar ALE of MgO:LN**, **2024** [6] | AFM roughness; device performance correlation [6] | Extending ALE cycles/chemistries and integrating into LN PIC flows as shown by **Chen et al. ALE** (2024) [6] |
| (4) Hard masks & alternative pattern transfer for deep/low‑loss LN | N/A | Deeply etched LN waveguide loss: **5.6 dB/m** (device outcome), **Tightly confining LN PICs and lasers**, **2022** (background age >18 mo) [7] | Propagation loss; compact bend performance; device demos [7] | Broader deployment of **DLC hard masks** (2023) and continued fully‑etched LN platform maturation (2023) [3][5] |
| (5) Post‑etch smoothing / chemo‑mechanical approaches | N/A | High‑Q outcome (metric noted qualitatively as “ultra‑high Q”): **(no numeric Q in items)**; method: PLACE + high‑T anneal, **Ultra‑high Q LN microring via PLACE**, **2023** [8] | Resonator Q; sidewall smoothness characterization [8] | Combining dry‑etch definition with **chemo‑mechanical** smoothing and annealing as per **Li et al. PLACE** (2023) [8] |

## Complementarity Map (mitigation “modalities”)

- **Redeposition suppression during ICP (process‑side)**: Dominates now via **ICP parameter tuning + layout/mask geometry** (e.g., higher DC bias/lower pressure; dense layouts; trapezoidal masks) in **Redeposition‑free ICP etching of LNOI (2023)** [2]. Overtaken when **post‑etch cleaning** must compensate for unavoidable redep in shared tools [4]. Limiting systematic: tool‑to‑tool plasma nonuniformity and redeposition variability [2][4].
- **Post‑etch wet cleaning (surface‑side)**: Dominates when residues are the main loss contributor; demonstrated with **RCA‑1 at 85 °C for 30 min (2024)** [4]. Overtaken when geometry integrity is limiting (risk of chipping/shape change with over‑cleaning) and upstream etch needs to be cleaner [4]. Limiting systematic: over‑clean damage vs residue removal trade‑off [4].
- **ALE (damage‑minimizing etch modality)**: Dominates for fine control and roughness reduction (e.g., **30% roughness reduction (2024)**) [6]. Overtaken for very high throughput/deep etches where conventional ICP with hard masks is faster [5][7]. Limiting systematic: integration complexity/throughput vs conventional ICP (not quantified in items) [6].
- **Hard‑mask enabled deep etch (materials‑side)**: Dominates for deep, tight confinement with low loss (e.g., **4 dB/m (2023)**; **5.6 dB/m (2022, background)**) [5][7]. Overtaken when post‑etch surface state/sub‑surface damage becomes the performance limiter rather than sidewall geometry (not quantified in items). Limiting systematic: long‑term stability/reliability of novel masks (explicit gap in items) [5][7].
- **Chemo‑mechanical smoothing + anneal (post‑processing modality)**: Dominates when ultimate resonator performance is set by sidewall scattering; shown as “ultra‑high Q” using PLACE + high‑T anneal (2023) [8]. Overtaken when process must remain fully dry and scalable without added mechanical steps (not quantified in items). Limiting systematic: added process complexity/scalability (not quantified in items) [8].

## Limiting Backgrounds & Systematics (and one mitigation each)

- **Redeposition on sidewalls in Ar‑based/physical ICP** increases roughness/scattering [2] → Mitigation: use redeposition‑minimizing ICP recipes (higher DC bias, lower pressure) and geometry/layout approaches (dense layouts, trapezoid masks) [2].
- **Residue removal vs geometry damage trade‑off** during wet cleaning (chipping/geometry change with excessive cleaning) [4] → Mitigation: adopt bounded cleaning windows demonstrated experimentally (RCA‑1 at 85 °C for 30 min; split orientations) and stop‑criteria metrology [4].
- **Insufficient standardization of post‑etch surface characterization** across labs [4][6] → Mitigation: routine AFM + chemistry checks (e.g., AFM/XPS/EDS as used in etch studies) to link process splits to roughness/chemistry outcomes [6][9].
- **Process portability across ICP systems/shared environments** is limited (explicit gap) [2][4] → Mitigation: publish tool‑agnostic “knob‑to‑outcome” maps and include post‑etch cleaning recipes validated for shared facilities [4].
- **Potential subsurface/defect damage impacting device performance** (not quantitatively established in items) → Mitigation: shift to more controlled removal (ALE) which demonstrated roughness reduction without added wet processing [6].

## 1) Suppress redeposition and roughness *during* plasma etching

**What the items quantify**
- Deeply etched, high‑density LN PIC flows with robust pattern transfer have demonstrated **4 dB/m** optical loss in **2023 (High density lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits)**, indicating the level achievable when etch + mask + residue control are successful [5].

**Mechanism and levers (from process studies)**
- Redeposition during ICP etching is highlighted as a primary damage mechanism degrading sidewall quality and increasing optical loss; reported mitigations include **increasing DC bias** and **lowering chamber pressure**, plus **dense layouts** (ion deflection) and **trapezoidal masks** to reduce sidewall redep, in **2023 (Redeposition‑free ICP etching of LNOI)** [2]. (The items do not provide numeric bias/pressure values; only the direction of change is stated.)

**Forward milestone (named, dated)**
- By **2024–2026**, a practical milestone is to reproduce “shared‑tool” low‑loss waveguides using the combined strategy: redeposition‑aware ICP recipe (per **Redeposition‑free ICP etching**, 2023) plus validated post‑etch cleans (per **Optimization of LNOI waveguide fabrication**, 2024) [2][4].

## 2) Post‑etch wet cleaning to remove redeposited material without damaging geometry

**What the items quantify**
- A systematic process optimization demonstrated a residue‑removal recipe: **RCA‑1 at 85 °C for 30 min (2024, Optimization of waveguide fabrication processes in LNOI photonics)**, performed as two **15 min** steps with different sample orientations, to remove redeposition from waveguide sidewalls [4]. The same study reports that overly aggressive cleaning can cause **chipping** and geometry changes (qualitative) [4].

**Practical mitigation stack (as described in items)**
- Combine: (i) careful ICP parameter tuning, (ii) hard‑mask choice (Cr vs SiO2 discussed), and (iii) bounded post‑etch cleaning to prevent trading residue for geometry damage [4].

**Forward milestone (named, dated)**
- In **2024–2027**, adopt the **2024 optimization protocol** as a baseline and quantify (in your own flow) the process window where RCA‑1 removes redeposition but does not measurably alter waveguide width/sidewall angle (metrology approach referenced in the same study) [4].

## 3) Atomic layer etching (ALE) to reduce roughness/damage burden

**What the items quantify**
- **30%** sidewall/surface roughness reduction was achieved **without additional wet processing** using isotropic ALE of MgO‑doped LN with sequential **H2** and **SF6/Ar** plasmas in **2024 (Isotropic atomic layer etching of MgO:LN)** [6].

**How this mitigates “damage after plasma etch”**
- If your damage mode is dominated by plasma‑generated roughness/residue that later must be cleaned, ALE offers a route to reduce the roughness at the etch step, potentially lowering how aggressive post‑etch cleaning needs to be (inference limited to what is stated: roughness reduction and no added wet processing) [6].

**Forward milestone (named, dated)**
- By **2025–2028**, evaluate ALE insertion for critical surfaces/sidewalls where scattering dominates, using the **2024 H2 + SF6/Ar ALE** sequence as the named baseline and tracking whether you can relax post‑etch cleaning intensity while meeting roughness targets [6].

## 4) Alternative pattern transfer (hard masks; chemo‑mechanical smoothing) to reduce etch‑induced damage sensitivity

**What the items quantify**
- Deep‑etch, tight‑confinement processes with DLC hard masks demonstrated **5.6 dB/m** loss in **2022 (Tightly confining LN PICs and lasers)** (background, >18 months) [7].
- A fully etched/compact LN PIC platform reported **8.5 dB/m** propagation loss in **2023 (Compact lithium niobate photonic integrated circuits)** [3].

**Mitigation logic supported by items**
- Robust hard masks (notably DLC) are repeatedly associated with enabling deeper etches with low loss and higher integration density, implying better control of sidewall quality and/or reduced process variability at depth [3][5][7].
- PLACE + high‑temperature annealing is reported to improve sidewall smoothness and yield “ultra‑high Q” microrings (no numeric Q value provided in items, so not stated quantitatively) [8].

**Forward milestone (named, dated)**
- By **2026–2030**, combine deep‑etch hard‑mask flows (e.g., DLC‑enabled approaches cited in **2023 high‑density LN PICs**) with a post‑definition smoothing step (PLACE + high‑T anneal, **2023**) for resonator‑limited circuits, and benchmark against the **2023: 4 dB/m** loss level as a device‑level target [5][8].
